quote: I just have a few questions for you, and then we'll be able to get you a quote for your home.
stewart_read: Carter frames the appointment as quote delivery before the verify section is even complete. This is an expectation-framing error, not scope creep — Carter is not explaining solar, but is promising a deliverable (a quote) that belongs to the closer's proposal stage. The error is reactive: no evidence Carter is deliberately setting up a later reframe.
alternative_hypothesis: Carter is using 'quote' as casual shorthand for 'appointment' and intends to correct the framing later — but there is no evidence of a planned correction anywhere in the transcript.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, pull this line and ask Carter what Jennifer thought she was agreeing to. The correct frame at this stage is 'a 30-minute call where our specialist builds a design specific to your home' — not a quote. The button_up section's language ('I blocked off that time for an hour to ensure you don't feel rushed') exists precisely to set this expectation. Carter skipped it.

quote: is there anyone else on the title of the home with you?
stewart_read: Jennifer's answer is ambiguous — she describes a co-title holder but concludes 'I'm the owner' (02:01). Carter accepts this self-identification without confirming whether the other title holder needs to be present for a decision. Per protocols.spouse_decision and protocols.adult_child_decision_maker, anyone on the title is a co-decision-maker who should be included in the appointment. Carter did not probe whether this person would need to be involved.
alternative_hypothesis: Jennifer's explicit 'I'm the owner' statement is clear enough that probing further would feel intrusive — Carter made a reasonable judgment call to accept it.
coaching_implication: The schema is explicit: anyone on the title should be on the appointment. Jennifer's explanation was complicated enough that a single follow-up — 'Is that person involved in decisions about the home?' — would have either confirmed Carter's read or surfaced a co-decision-maker risk. Ask Carter in the 1-on-1: what happens if the other title holder objects after the closer's proposal?

quote: I was just curious because because I needed I needed a quote and to see, you know, what kind of investment you have to put in initially and what type you get back later.
stewart_read: Jennifer is articulating the exact ROI framing that makes a solar pitch land — she wants to know cost-in vs. value-back. This is not an objection; it is an invitation. Carter's response ('Yeah. I do. I just yeah. And I'll actually introduce some new programs') is a vague reassurance that neither anchors her framing nor creates curiosity about her specific numbers. The flip available here: her bill pain (even at $50-60/month summer, with a projected winter spike) is the 'investment you're already making' — the closer's design will show her what she gets back. Carter had the raw material and did not use it.
coaching_implication: This is the call's clearest missed curiosity-creation moment. In the 1-on-1, ask Carter: 'What did you know about Jennifer's bill at this point?' Carter knew she was paying $50-60 in summer with a projected winter increase. A suggested rephrase (intent, not verbatim): 'That's exactly what the specialist will show you — your specific numbers, based on your home. Most people are surprised by what they're already spending.' The goal is to make Jennifer curious about her own math, not to promise a deliverable.

quote: And with all the government changes going on, I ain't counting on them at all.
stewart_read: Jennifer is expressing skepticism about government incentives — a real and common concern. The correct move here is a clean deferral that also creates curiosity: the specialist handles incentive questions, and the design will show what the numbers look like with or without them. Carter's response ('I'll actually introduce some new programs that will be a guide to go over with you tomorrow') is vague and slightly misleading — it sounds like Carter is promising to personally present programs, when a specialist is coming. The primary coaching frame is deferral-with-poor-execution, not a missed flip. Carter correctly avoided going into incentive detail (scope discipline), but the deferral did not create any curiosity or anchor Jennifer's attention on what the appointment will actually show her.
alternative_hypothesis: Carter walked past a flip opportunity — Jennifer's skepticism about government incentives could have been inverted ('The programs that don't depend on government are the ones worth looking at — that's what the specialist will show you'). This read is plausible but secondary; the more grounded coaching gap is the vague deferral, not the absence of a flip.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, separate two things: (1) Carter was right not to go into incentive detail — that is the specialist's job. (2) The deferral language was vague and slightly inaccurate. A suggested rephrase (intent, not verbatim): 'Totally — the specialist will show you what the numbers look like regardless of incentives, so you can make the call based on what's guaranteed.' That defers correctly AND creates a reason to show up.

quote: Have the brochures and prices ready, and and I at least can look at them and and and think about it for a month or two till I figure out what's going on.
stewart_read: Jennifer closes in explicit browsing mode — 'think about it for a month or two.' Carter's response is 'Yeah. For sure.' — accepting the posture without any curiosity bridge or appointment reframe. This is the call's most consequential missed moment: the booking is real, but Jennifer has mentally categorized it as low-stakes. The closer inherits this framing unless the handoff brief flags it.
alternative_hypothesis: Carter accepts the browsing framing deliberately to avoid pressure that might cause Jennifer to cancel — a soft-close preservation move. Possible, but there is no evidence of a planned closer-side reframe, and the button_up protocol's 'is that fair' handshake was not used to create any commitment.
coaching_implication: Pull this line in the 1-on-1 and ask Carter: 'What did Jennifer think she was agreeing to?' The button_up protocol exists to convert a booked slot into a felt commitment. Carter skipped the 'is that fair' handshake and the 'no driving, at your computer' anchor — both of which signal to the customer that this is a real appointment, not a brochure drop. Walk Carter through the full button_up sequence and connect it directly to show rate.

quote: well, since we just moved here, I don't know what the average here is going to be. It's summer right now, so we're probably paying anywhere between, I don't know, 50 and $60 because we're not using heat. This winter will probably be closer to maybe 100 or or or more.
stewart_read: Carter captures the bill range and the seasonal context. Jennifer even volunteers a projected winter increase. Carter's response is 'Oh. I hope that makes sense. Yeah. That's correct. When did you rent the home?' — the bill is captured and immediately dropped. No flip, no emotional alignment against the utility, no acknowledgment of the winter spike as a pain point. Per coaching_philosophy.bill_anchoring, capture alone is necessary but not sufficient.
coaching_implication: This is the floor-wide pattern the schema flags: bill captured, bill dropped. In the 1-on-1, ask Carter what Jennifer's winter bill means for the pitch. Jennifer volunteered the projection herself — that is an unusually clean opening for a bill flip. A suggested rephrase (intent, not verbatim): 'So you're already looking at $100-plus this winter just for electricity — and that's with gas handling the heat. That's exactly the kind of number the specialist will work from.' The goal is to make the bill feel like a problem worth solving before the appointment.
